text: Two metals A and B having similar ionic radii react with oxygen to give only monoxides and react with nitrogen to give nitrides. Then A and B respectively are:
explanation:
**Li‚Āļ** (Group 1) and **Mg¬≤‚Āļ** (Group 2) have similar ionic radii due to their **diagonal relationship** in the periodic table.

- Both Li and Mg form only **monoxides** (Li‚āāO and MgO) ‚ÄĒ unlike other alkali metals which form peroxides or superoxides.
- Both react with nitrogen to form **nitrides** (Li‚āÉN and Mg‚āÉN‚āā).

This diagonal relationship between Li and Mg accounts for their similar chemical behaviour.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 1

text: $$\text{BF}_3$$ is used as a catalyst in several industrial processes due to its:
explanation:
**BF‚āÉ** is used as a catalyst due to its **strong Lewis acid** nature.

In BF‚āÉ:
- Boron is **sp¬≤ hybridised** with only 6 electrons in its valence shell (2 electrons short of an octet)
- This electron deficiency makes BF‚āÉ a strong Lewis acid ‚ÄĒ it readily accepts a lone pair of electrons
- It acts as a Lewis acid catalyst in Friedel-Crafts reactions, polymerisation reactions, and other industrial processes

BF‚āÉ has no reducing properties.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 2

text: Among the following oxides, which one is weakly basic and yet a powerful oxidant?
explanation:
**PbO‚āā** is weakly basic and a powerful oxidising agent.

Among Group 14 dioxides (CO‚āā, SiO‚āā, GeO‚āā, PbO‚āā):
- **Basic nature increases down the group**: PbO‚āā is the most basic among them
- **Pb is in +4 oxidation state** in PbO‚āā, but due to the **inert pair effect**, Pb¬≤‚Āļ is more stable than Pb‚Āī‚Āļ
- Therefore, PbO‚āā has a strong tendency to be reduced from Pb‚Āī‚Āļ ‚Üí Pb¬≤‚Āļ (PbO), making it a **powerful oxidising agent**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 3

text: Name the kind of silicate formed when two silicate units $$(\text{SiO}_4)^{4-}$$ share one oxygen atom.
explanation:
When **two** tetrahedral silicate units $$[\text{SiO}_4]^{4-}$$ share **one** oxygen atom, a **pyrosilicate** (also called disilicate) is formed:

$$[\text{Si}_2\text{O}_7]^{6-}$$

The shared oxygen bridges the two Si atoms:
$$^-\text{O}_3\text{Si}‚ÄĒ\text{O}‚ÄĒ\text{SiO}_3^-$$

- **Pyrosilicate:** 2 SiO‚āĄ units share 1 oxygen ‚Üí $$[\text{Si}_2\text{O}_7]^{6-}$$
- **Chain silicate:** Multiple units share 2 oxygens
- **Ring silicate:** Units share 2 oxygens in a cyclic pattern
- **Sheet silicate:** Units share 3 oxygens
name: s and p Block Elements Item 4

text: $$\text{SF}_6$$ is a kinetically inert substance because:
options: Greater difference in electronegativities of S and F
explanation:
**SF‚āÜ** is kinetically inert because it is **sterically protected**.

In SF‚āÜ:
- Sulphur is **sp¬≥d¬≤** hybridised
- The geometry and shape are **octahedral** (perfectly symmetrical)
- All six F atoms completely surround the central S atom, creating a **sterically shielded** environment
- The dipole moment őľ = 0 (non-polar molecule)
- No attacking reagent can access the central S atom due to the six surrounding F atoms

This steric protection, not just electronegativity difference, is why SF‚āÜ is kinetically inert.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 5

text: A compound X of boron reacts with NH‚āÉ on heating to give compound Y, which is called inorganic benzene. X can be prepared by treating BF‚āÉ with lithium aluminium hydride. X and Y are:
explanation:
**Step 1:** BF‚āÉ is treated with LiAlH‚āĄ:
$$4\text{BF}_3 + 3\text{LiAlH}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 3\text{LiF} + 3\text{AlF}_3$$

So **X = B‚āāH‚āÜ (diborane)**

**Step 2:** Diborane reacts with excess NH‚āÉ on heating:
$$3\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{B}_3\text{N}_3\text{H}_6 + 12\text{H}_2$$

So **Y = B‚āÉN‚āÉH‚āÜ (borazine or inorganic benzene)**

Borazine is isoelectronic and isostructural with benzene, with alternating B and N atoms in the ring.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 6

text: How many compounds can be formed when carbon combines with oxygen?
explanation:
When carbon combines with oxygen, **four** oxides (oxocarbons) can be formed:

1. **Carbon monoxide** ‚Üí CO
2. **Carbon dioxide** ‚Üí CO‚āā
3. **Carbon suboxide** ‚Üí C‚āÉO‚āā
4. **Mellitic anhydride** ‚Üí C‚āĀ‚āāO‚āČ

These four compounds represent the different ways carbon and oxygen can combine in various stoichiometric ratios and molecular structures.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 7

text: Gas X is obtained on heating KClO‚āÉ with catalyst MnO‚āā. This gas (in excess) on reaction with white phosphorus forms an acidic oxide Y. Y on dissolving in water forms compound Z. Identify X, Y and Z:
explanation:
**Step 1:** KClO‚āÉ with MnO‚āā catalyst on heating:
$$2\text{KClO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{MnO}_2, \Delta} 2\text{KCl} + 3\text{O}_2 \uparrow$$
So **X = O‚āā**

**Step 2:** Excess O‚āā with white phosphorus:
$$\text{P}_4 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$$
So **Y = P‚āāO‚āÖ** (phosphorus pentoxide, acidic oxide)

**Step 3:** P‚āāO‚āÖ dissolved in water:
$$\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$$
So **Z = H‚āÉPO‚āĄ** (orthophosphoric acid)
name: s and p Block Elements Item 8

text: The number of different bonds present in $$\text{P}_4\text{O}_{10}$$ is:
explanation:
In the structure of **P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ**:

- There are **4 phosphorus atoms**, each with **1 terminal P=O bond** ‚Üí 4 P=O double bonds
- The 4 P atoms are linked by oxygen bridges (P‚ÄďO‚ÄďP), contributing **12 P‚ÄďO single bonds** (each P forms 3 bridging P‚ÄďO bonds)

Total:
- **P‚ÄďO single bonds = 12** (bridging)
- **P=O double bonds = 4** (terminal)

P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ is a powerful desiccant and dehydrating agent, used to remove water from substances.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 9

text: Which of the following compounds are formed when boron trichloride is treated with water?
explanation:
When boron trichloride (BCl‚āÉ) is treated with water, it undergoes complete hydrolysis:

$$\text{BCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3 + 3\text{HCl}$$

Products:
- **H‚āÉBO‚āÉ** (boric acid / orthoboric acid) ‚ÄĒ a weak Lewis acid
- **HCl** (hydrochloric acid)

This is in contrast to CCl‚āĄ, which does not undergo hydrolysis due to the absence of vacant d-orbitals on carbon.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 10

text: When SbF‚āÖ reacts with XeF‚āĄ to form an adduct, the shapes of the cation and anion in the adduct respectively are:
explanation:
When **XeF‚āĄ** reacts with **SbF‚āÖ**, XeF‚āĄ acts as a fluoride (F‚ĀĽ) donor:
$$\text{XeF}_4 + \text{SbF}_5 \rightarrow [\text{XeF}_3]^+ [\text{SbF}_6]^-$$

**Cation [XeF‚āÉ]‚Āļ:**
- Xe has 3 bond pairs + 2 lone pairs ‚Üí sp¬≥d hybridisation
- Shape = **T-shaped**

**Anion [SbF‚āÜ]‚ĀĽ:**
- Sb has 6 bond pairs + 0 lone pairs ‚Üí sp¬≥d¬≤ hybridisation
- Shape = **Octahedral**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 11

text: Identify the products formed when chlorine reacts with cold and dilute sodium hydroxide solution.
explanation:
When Cl‚āā reacts with **cold and dilute NaOH**:
$$\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\text{cold, dilute}} \text{NaCl} + \text{NaOCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Products:
- **NaCl** (sodium chloride)
- **NaOCl** (sodium hypochlorite ‚ÄĒ used in bleaching)
- **H‚āāO**

Note: With **hot and concentrated** NaOH, the products are different:
$$3\text{Cl}_2 + 6\text{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\text{hot, conc.}} 5\text{NaCl} + \text{NaClO}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
name: s and p Block Elements Item 12

text: Choose the alkali metal with the highest reactivity among the following:
explanation:
Among alkali metals (Group 1), reactivity **increases down the group** because:
- Ionisation energy decreases
- Atomic size increases
- The outermost electron is farther from the nucleus and more easily lost

**Caesium (Cs)** is the most reactive alkali metal (excluding radioactive Fr) because it has the **lowest first ionisation energy** among the stable alkali metals.

Order of reactivity: Li < Na < K < Rb < **Cs**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 13

text: Which oxide among the following cannot act as a reducing agent?
explanation:
An oxide **cannot act as a reducing agent** when the central atom is already in its **highest possible oxidation state** and cannot be oxidised further.

- **SO‚āā:** S is in +4 state; Group 16 max = +6 ‚Üí can be oxidised ‚Üí **can act as reducing agent**
- **NO‚āā:** N is in +4 state; Group 15 max = +5 ‚Üí can be oxidised ‚Üí **can act as reducing agent**
- **CO‚āā:** C is in +4 state; Group 14 max = +4 ‚Üí **cannot be oxidised further** ‚Üí **CANNOT act as reducing agent** ‚úď
- **ClO‚āā:** Cl is in +4 state; Group 17 max = +7 ‚Üí can be oxidised ‚Üí **can act as reducing agent**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 14

text: Dilute sodium hydroxide does not react with which of the following?
explanation:
**Boron (B)** does not react with **dilute NaOH**.

- **Al and Ga** readily react with dilute NaOH to form soluble aluminate/gallate complexes and H‚āā gas
- **Ti** reacts with concentrated NaOH
- **B** only reacts with **concentrated NaOH**:
$$\text{B} + \text{NaOH (conc.)} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Na[B(OH)}_4\text{]} + \dfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2$$

Boron's resistance to dilute NaOH is due to its strong, non-metallic covalent structure and very high melting point.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 15

text: Which species among the following does NOT show disproportionation reactions?
explanation:
**Disproportionation** is a redox reaction where the same species is simultaneously oxidised AND reduced.

For disproportionation to occur, the central atom must have the ability to both increase AND decrease its oxidation state.

Oxidation states of Cl in the given species:
- ClO‚ĀĽ: Cl = +1 ‚Üí can go to ‚ąí1 or higher ‚Üí disproportionates ‚úď
- ClO‚āā‚ĀĽ: Cl = +3 ‚Üí can go to ‚ąí1 or higher ‚Üí disproportionates ‚úď
- ClO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ: Cl = +5 ‚Üí can go to ‚ąí1 or higher ‚Üí disproportionates ‚úď
- **ClO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ:** Cl = **+7** ‚Üí this is the **maximum** oxidation state of Cl ‚Üí **cannot be oxidised further** ‚Üí **cannot disproportionate** ‚úó
name: s and p Block Elements Item 16

text: Concentrated HNO‚āÉ turns brown on standing due to the formation of:
explanation:
Concentrated HNO‚āÉ slowly **decomposes** on standing (especially in light) according to:

$$4\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

**NO‚āā** (nitrogen dioxide) is a **reddish-brown gas** that dissolves in the nitric acid, giving it the characteristic **yellow-brown colour**.

This is why concentrated HNO‚āÉ is stored in brown bottles to prevent light-induced decomposition.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 17

text: Which of the following represents the disproportionation of potassium chlorate?
explanation:
**Disproportionation** requires the same element to be simultaneously oxidised and reduced.

$$4\text{KClO}_3 \rightarrow 3\text{KClO}_4 + \text{KCl}$$

Oxidation states of Cl:
- In KClO‚āÉ: Cl = **+5**
- In KClO‚āĄ: Cl = **+7** (oxidised)
- In KCl: Cl = **‚ąí1** (reduced)

Since Cl (+5) is simultaneously oxidised to +7 AND reduced to ‚ąí1, this is a true **disproportionation reaction**. It occurs at higher temperatures.

Note: Option (a) is a simple decomposition (not disproportionation) since Cl stays at ‚ąí1 throughout.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 18

text: A pungent smelling gas A gives dense white fumes with conc. HCl. When A reacts with an alkaline solution of a colourless reagent B, a reddish-brown precipitate C is formed. The compounds A, B, C respectively are:
explanation:
**A = NH‚āÉ (ammonia):**
- Pungent smell ‚úď
- Reacts with HCl to give dense white fumes of NH‚āĄCl:
$$\text{NH}_3(g) + \text{HCl}(g) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(s) \ \text{(white fumes)}$$

**B = K‚āāHgI‚āĄ (Nessler's reagent):**
- A colourless alkaline solution (contains KOH)

**C = Iodide of Million's base (reddish-brown precipitate):**
- When NH‚āÉ reacts with Nessler's reagent under alkaline conditions, it forms a characteristic **reddish-brown precipitate** ‚ÄĒ iodide of Million's base $$[\text{Hg}_2\text{N}]\text{I} \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- This reaction is used as the **Nessler's test** for ammonia
name: s and p Block Elements Item 19

text: The possible oxidation states of Group 13 elements are:
explanation:
The general electronic configuration of Group 13 elements is **ns¬≤ np¬Ļ**.

They can show **+3** (using all 3 valence electrons) and **+1** (using only the np¬Ļ electron, leaving the ns¬≤ pair unused).

The **+1 state** becomes increasingly common down the group due to the **inert pair effect** ‚ÄĒ the ns¬≤ electrons become progressively less involved in bonding as we go from B to Tl:
- B, Al: predominantly +3
- Ga, In: both +1 and +3
- Tl: predominantly **+1** (Tl‚Āļ is more stable than Tl¬≥‚Āļ)

The +2 state is not a general oxidation state for Group 13.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 20

text: Which of the following mineral acids can attack $$\text{SiO}_2$$?
explanation:
**Hydrofluoric acid (HF)** is the only common acid that attacks **SiO‚āā**:

$$\text{SiO}_2 + 4\text{HF} \rightarrow \text{SiF}_4\uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
or
$$\text{SiO}_2 + 6\text{HF} \rightarrow \text{H}_2[\text{SiF}_6] + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Although HF is a **weak acid**, it reacts with SiO‚āā very effectively because of the **strong Si‚ÄďF bond** formation (Si‚ÄďF bond is one of the strongest bonds).

HNO‚āÉ, H‚āāSO‚āĄ, and HCl do **not** react with SiO‚āā. This property of HF is used to **etch glass** (which contains SiO‚āā).
name: s and p Block Elements Item 21

text: Which one of the following is NOT present in the nitration mixture?
explanation:
The **nitration mixture** is a 1:1 mixture of conc. HNO‚āÉ and conc. H‚āāSO‚āĄ.

The reaction in the nitrating mixture generates the electrophilic nitronium ion:
$$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ + \text{HSO}_4^-$$
$$\text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Species present in the nitration mixture:
- **$$\text{NO}_2^+$$** (nitronium ion ‚ÄĒ the active electrophile) ‚úď
- **$$\text{HSO}_4^-$$** ‚úď
- **$$\text{H}_2\text{O}$$** ‚úď
- **$$\text{SO}_4^{2-}$$** ‚ÄĒ NOT present, because H‚āāSO‚āĄ only partially ionises to give HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ in this concentrated mixture ‚úó
name: s and p Block Elements Item 22

text:
When Xe is taken in excess, what product is formed in the reaction:
$$\text{Xe}(g) + \text{F}_2(g) \rightarrow ?$$
explanation:
The product formed depends on the **Xe:F‚āā ratio** and conditions:

| Conditions | Product |
|---|---|
| Xe in **excess**, 673 K, 1 bar | **XeF‚āā** |
| 1:1 ratio, 673 K, 1 bar | XeF‚āĄ |
| F‚āā in excess, 573 K, 60-70 bar | XeF‚āÜ |

When **excess Xe** reacts with F‚āā:
$$\text{Xe (excess)} + \text{F}_2 \xrightarrow{673 \ \text{K}, \ 1 \ \text{bar}} \text{XeF}_2$$

**XeF‚āā** is formed ‚ÄĒ it is a linear molecule (sp¬≥d hybridisation, 3 lone pairs on Xe).
name: s and p Block Elements Item 23

text: The formula of white phosphorus in the vapour state is:
explanation:
**White phosphorus** (also called yellow phosphorus) exists as **P‚āĄ molecules** in both the solid state and the vapour state.

The P‚āĄ molecule has a **tetrahedral structure** with:
- 4 phosphorus atoms at the corners of a tetrahedron
- Each P atom forms 3 P‚ÄďP single bonds
- The P‚ÄďP‚ÄďP bond angle is ~60¬į, which creates significant angular strain

This angular strain is responsible for the **high reactivity** of white phosphorus.

At very high temperatures (above ~800¬įC), P‚āĄ begins to dissociate into P‚āā.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 24

text: A chemical A is used for the preparation of washing soda to recover ammonia. When CO‚āā is bubbled through an aqueous solution of A, the solution turns milky. It is used in white washing due to its disinfectant nature. What is the chemical formula of A?
explanation:
All clues point to **Ca(OH)‚āā (calcium hydroxide / slaked lime)**:

**Clue 1 ‚ÄĒ Ammonia recovery in soda process:**
$$2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Clue 2 ‚ÄĒ Turns milky with CO‚āā:**
$$\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
(White precipitate of CaCO‚āÉ makes the solution milky ‚ÄĒ this is the **limewater test** for CO‚āā)

**Clue 3 ‚ÄĒ Used in white washing:** Ca(OH)‚āā is used as a disinfectant and white pigment in whitewashing walls.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 25

text: The correct order of increasing ionic character among LiH, NaH, KH, RbH, CsH is:
explanation:
According to **Fajan's rules**, ionic character of a bond increases as:
- The cation size increases
- The charge on cation is lower

Down Group 1 (Li ‚Üí Cs), the **cation size increases** (Li‚Āļ < Na‚Āļ < K‚Āļ < Rb‚Āļ < Cs‚Āļ), which leads to **less polarisation** of the hydride anion and therefore **greater ionic character**.

**Caesium hydride (CsH)** has the most ionic character because Cs‚Āļ is the largest cation with the lowest charge density.

Correct order: **LiH < NaH < KH < RbH < CsH**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 26

text: Which halogen can be oxidised by concentrated nitric acid?
explanation:
**Reducing power** of halogens increases down the group: F < Cl < Br < **I**

**Iodine** is the only halogen that can be **oxidised** by concentrated nitric acid:
$$\text{I}_2 + 10\text{HNO}_3(\text{conc.}) \rightarrow 2\text{HIO}_3 + 10\text{NO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Iodine is oxidised to **iodic acid (HIO‚āÉ)** where I is in +5 oxidation state.

**Fluorine** is the strongest oxidising agent ‚ÄĒ it cannot be oxidised (it has no positive oxidation states). Cl and Br are not easily oxidised by HNO‚āÉ.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 27

text: Which of the following statements about diborane (B‚āāH‚āÜ) is incorrect?
options: The 2 bridged hydrogen atoms and 2 boron atoms lie in one plane
Out of 6 B‚ÄďH bonds, two bonds can be described as 3-centre 2-electron bonds
Out of 6 B‚ÄďH bonds, four bonds can be described as 3-centre 2-electron bonds
The four terminal B‚ÄďH bonds are 2-centre 2-electron regular bonds
explanation:
In **B‚āāH‚āÜ (diborane)**:

- There are **4 terminal B‚ÄďH bonds** ‚ÄĒ normal 2-centre 2-electron bonds ‚úď
- There are **2 bridging B‚ÄďH‚ÄďB bonds** ‚ÄĒ these are **3-centre 2-electron (3c-2e)** bonds (banana bonds) ‚úď
- The 2 bridging H atoms and 2 B atoms lie in one plane ‚úď

**Statement (c) is incorrect:** Only **2** B‚ÄďH bonds (the bridging ones) are 3c-2e bonds, not four. The four **terminal** B‚ÄďH bonds are normal 2c-2e bonds.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 28

text: The correct order of acidic character of Group 16 hydrides is:
explanation:
**Acidic character** of hydrides increases down Group 16 because:
- Atomic size increases down the group
- **M‚ÄďH bond strength decreases** (O‚ÄďH > S‚ÄďH > Se‚ÄďH > Te‚ÄďH)
- Weaker M‚ÄďH bond ‚Üí easier loss of H‚Āļ ‚Üí greater acidic character

Hence the correct order is:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O} < \text{H}_2\text{S} < \text{H}_2\text{Se} < \text{H}_2\text{Te}$$

Note: H‚āāO has the weakest acidic character despite having the strongest O‚ÄďH bond, because the extra stability due to hydrogen bonding makes it harder to dissociate.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 29

text: The correct order comparing the acid strengths of HF, HCl, HBr and HI is:
explanation:
Acid strength of hydrogen halides **increases down the group**:

**Reason:** As we go from HF to HI:
- Atomic size of halogen increases
- **H‚ÄďX bond length increases** and **H‚ÄďX bond strength decreases**
- Weaker bond ‚Üí easier release of H‚Āļ ‚Üí stronger acid

Bond dissociation energies: H‚ÄďF (569) > H‚ÄďCl (431) > H‚ÄďBr (368) > H‚ÄďI (297) kJ/mol

Correct order of acid strength:
$$\text{HF} << \text{HCl} << \text{HBr} << \text{HI}$$

Note: **HF is a weak acid** (only partially dissociates) while HCl, HBr, and HI are all strong acids.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 30

text: Which of the following metal is used as a catalyst in Haber's process of ammonia synthesis?
explanation:
In the **Haber process** for industrial synthesis of ammonia:
$$\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$$

**Conditions:**
- **Catalyst:** Finely divided **iron (Fe)** with promoters (Al‚āāO‚āÉ and K‚āāO)
- **Temperature:** 450‚Äď500¬įC
- **Pressure:** ~200 atm

Iron catalyst provides a suitable surface for the adsorption and activation of N‚āā and H‚āā molecules, achieving an acceptable rate of reaction while keeping the yield reasonable.

Without the catalyst, the reaction is too slow even at high temperature.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 31

text: $$\text{O}_3 + 2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow ?$$
explanation:
Ozone is a strong oxidising agent and oxidises aqueous potassium iodide:

$$2\text{KI}(aq) + \text{O}_3(g) \rightarrow \text{I}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{KOH}$$

Ozone oxidises **I‚ĀĽ** to **I‚āā** (iodine).

This reaction is used as a **qualitative test for ozone**: When ozone is passed through KI solution, the liberated I‚āā turns starch paper **blue-black**. This confirms the presence of ozone and distinguishes it from ordinary oxygen, which cannot oxidise I‚ĀĽ under normal conditions.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 32

text: Which of the following does NOT exist?
explanation:
**NeF‚āā does not exist** because:

- The sum of the **first and second ionisation enthalpies** of Ne is much higher than those of Xe
- F‚āā can oxidise Xe to Xe¬≤‚Āļ (to form XeF‚āā), but **cannot oxidise Ne to Ne¬≤‚Āļ** due to the extremely high ionisation energy of Ne
- Also, Ne has **no available d-orbitals** and its very compact electron configuration makes bond formation with F essentially impossible

All xenon compounds given (XeO‚āÉ, XeOF‚āĄ, XeF‚āÜ) are known and have been synthesised. Xenon has lower ionisation energies than neon, making its chemistry possible.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 33

text: Which of the following statements is NOT correct?
options: From SO‚āā to TeO‚āā, reducing power decreases
The order of boiling points of Group 16 hydrides is: H‚āāS < H‚āāSe < H‚āāTe < H‚āāO
Rhombic sulphur has S‚āą molecules while monoclinic sulphur has S‚āÜ molecules
The bond angle in the ozone molecule is 117¬į
explanation:
**Statement (c) is incorrect:** Both **rhombic sulphur** and **monoclinic sulphur** contain **S‚āą molecules** ‚ÄĒ they differ only in the crystalline arrangement (packing) of the S‚āą rings, not in the molecular formula.

All other statements are correct:
- **(a):** Reducing power decreases from SO‚āā to TeO‚āā going down the group ‚úď
- **(b):** H‚āāO has anomalously high boiling point due to H-bonding, so: H‚āāS < H‚āāSe < H‚āāTe < H‚āāO ‚úď
- **(d):** The bond angle in O‚āÉ is ~116.8¬į ‚Čą 117¬į ‚úď
name: s and p Block Elements Item 34

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

I. B‚āāH‚āÜ is an electron deficient hydride.
II. NH‚āÉ is an electron rich hydride.
III. NaH is a covalent hydride.
IV. YbH‚āā.‚āÖ‚āÖ is an interstitial hydride.
explanation:
**Statement I ‚ÄĒ Correct:** B‚āāH‚āÜ (diborane) is an **electron deficient hydride** ‚ÄĒ it has 3-centre-2-electron bonds because boron has only 6 electrons (not enough for normal 2c-2e bonds for all hydrogens). ‚úď

**Statement II ‚ÄĒ Correct:** NH‚āÉ is an **electron rich hydride** ‚ÄĒ it has a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen, making it electron-rich and a good Lewis base. ‚úď

**Statement III ‚ÄĒ Incorrect:** NaH is an **ionic hydride** (saline hydride), not a covalent hydride. Alkali metal hydrides are ionic (Na‚ĀļH‚ĀĽ). ‚úó

**Statement IV ‚ÄĒ Correct:** YbH‚āā.‚āÖ‚āÖ is an **interstitial hydride** ‚ÄĒ hydrogen atoms occupy the interstitial voids in the Yb metal lattice, forming a non-stoichiometric compound. ‚úď
name: s and p Block Elements Item 35

text:
In the following reactions, X, Y and Z are respectively:

$$3\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} X + 12\text{H}_2$$

$$6\text{NaH} + 2\text{BF}_3 \xrightarrow{450 \ \text{K}} Y + 6\text{NaF}$$

$$\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow Z + 6\text{H}_2$$
explanation:
**Reaction 1:** Diborane + excess NH‚āÉ on heating ‚Üí **X = B‚āÉN‚āÉH‚āÜ (borazine/inorganic benzene)** + H‚āā ‚úď

**Reaction 2:** NaH + BF‚āÉ at 450 K (industrial method for diborane):
$$6\text{NaH} + 2\text{BF}_3 \xrightarrow{450 \ \text{K}} \text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NaF}$$
**Y = B‚āāH‚āÜ (diborane)** ‚úď

**Reaction 3:** Diborane hydrolysis:
$$\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_3\text{BO}_3 + 6\text{H}_2$$
**Z = H‚āÉBO‚āÉ (boric acid / orthoboric acid)** ‚úď

Therefore: X = B‚āÉN‚āÉH‚āÜ, Y = B‚āāH‚āÜ, Z = H‚āÉBO‚āÉ
name: s and p Block Elements Item 36

text:
**Assertion (A):** P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ cannot be used to remove moisture from ammonia gas.

**Reason (R):** P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ reacts with NH‚āÉ gas.

The correct answer is:
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
explanation:
**Assertion (A) is correct:** P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ cannot be used as a desiccant (drying agent) for ammonia gas.

**Reason (R) is correct and explains (A):** P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ chemically reacts with NH‚āÉ:
$$12\text{NH}_3 + \text{P}_4\text{O}_{10} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4(\text{NH}_4)_3\text{PO}_4$$

Since P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ reacts with NH‚āÉ (rather than just absorbing water from it), it would destroy the ammonia. Therefore it cannot be used to dry NH‚āÉ gas.

Correct drying agents for NH‚āÉ are quicklime (CaO) or solid NaOH, which don't react with NH‚āÉ.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 37

text: Which one of the following statements regarding helium is NOT correct?
options: It is used to fill gas balloons instead of hydrogen because it is lighter and not flammable
It is used in gas-cooled nuclear reactors
It is used to produce and sustain powerful superconducting magnets
It is not used as a cryogenic agent
explanation:
**Statement (d) is incorrect:** **Liquid helium IS used as a cryogenic agent** ‚ÄĒ it is one of the most important cryogenic fluids, with a boiling point of **‚ąí269¬įC (4 K)**, the lowest of any substance.

Liquid helium is used to:
- Cool superconducting magnets in MRI machines
- Maintain ultra-low temperatures in research laboratories

All other statements are correct:
- He is used in balloons (lighter than air, non-flammable, unlike H‚āā) ‚úď
- Used as a coolant in nuclear reactors ‚úď
- Used to maintain superconducting magnets ‚úď
name: s and p Block Elements Item 38

text: KO‚āā reacts with water to form A, B and C. B forms C when it reacts with iodine in basic medium. What are B and C respectively?
explanation:
**Step 1:** KO‚āā (potassium superoxide) reacts with water:
$$2\text{KO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{KOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

- A = KOH, **B = H‚āāO‚āā**, C = O‚āā

**Step 2:** B (H‚āāO‚āā) reacts with iodine in basic medium:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{I}_2 + 2\text{KOH} \rightarrow 2\text{KI} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2$$

Here H‚āāO‚āā acts as a **reducing agent** and is oxidised to **O‚āā (C)**.

KOH cannot reduce I‚āā, so **B = H‚āāO‚āā** and **C = O‚āā**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 39

text: Which one of the following methods is used to prepare carbon monoxide on a commercial scale?
options: Dehydration of formic acid with conc. H‚āāSO‚āĄ
Direct oxidation of C in limited supply of oxygen
explanation:
**Passing steam over hot coke** is used to prepare CO on a commercial scale (water gas process):

$$\text{C}(\text{coke}) + \text{H}_2\text{O (steam)} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni}, \Delta} \text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$$

This produces **water gas** (a mixture of CO and H‚āā), which is used industrially as a fuel and as a source of hydrogen.

Other options:
- (a) Dehydration of HCOOH with H‚āāSO‚āĄ gives CO, but not on commercial scale
- (b) Partial combustion of C gives CO but not used commercially
- (d) Heating CaCO‚āÉ gives CaO + CO‚āā (not CO)
name: s and p Block Elements Item 40

text: Chlorine oxidises SO‚āā in the presence of water to give an oxyacid A. Chlorine also oxidises I‚āā in the presence of water to give an oxyacid B. The oxidation states of S and I in A and B respectively are:
explanation:
**Reaction 1:** Cl‚āā oxidises SO‚āā in water:
$$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{SO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}$$
Oxyacid **A = H‚āāSO‚āĄ**
Oxidation state of S: $$x + 2(‚ąí1) + 4(‚ąí2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +6$$

**Reaction 2:** Cl‚āā oxidises I‚āā in water:
$$5\text{Cl}_2 + \text{I}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HIO}_3 + 10\text{HCl}$$
Oxyacid **B = HIO‚āÉ**
Oxidation state of I: $$1 + x + 3(‚ąí2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +5$$

Therefore, S in A = **+6**, I in B = **+5**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 41

text: White phosphorus is heated with concentrated NaOH in a CO‚āā atmosphere to form a gas A and compound B. When A is bubbled into aqueous CuSO‚āĄ solution, copper phosphide and C are formed. B and C are respectively:
explanation:
**Step 1:** White phosphorus + conc. NaOH in CO‚āā atmosphere:
$$\text{P}_4 + 3\text{NaOH} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{CO}_2, \Delta} \text{PH}_3(g) + 3\text{NaH}_2\text{PO}_2$$

- **A = PH‚āÉ** (phosphine gas ‚ÄĒ pungent smell)
- **B = NaH‚āāPO‚āā** (sodium hypophosphite)

**Step 2:** PH‚āÉ bubbled into CuSO‚āĄ solution:
$$2\text{PH}_3 + 3\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cu}_3\text{P}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

- Copper phosphide (Cu‚āÉP‚āā) is formed
- **C = H‚āāSO‚āĄ** (sulphuric acid)

Therefore: **B = NaH‚āāPO‚āā** and **C = H‚āāSO‚āĄ**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 42

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

I. Ga‚āāO‚āÉ is an amphoteric oxide.
II. The dimer of aluminium chloride has three Al‚ÄďCl‚ÄďAl bridge bonds.
III. Boron is a very hard refractory solid with a high melting temperature.
explanation:
**Statement I ‚ÄĒ Correct:** Ga‚āāO‚āÉ is **amphoteric** ‚ÄĒ it reacts with both acids and bases:
$$\text{Ga}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{GaCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{Ga}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{NaGaO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ‚úď

**Statement II ‚ÄĒ Incorrect:** The dimer of AlCl‚āÉ (Al‚āāCl‚āÜ) has only **two** Al‚ÄďCl‚ÄďAl bridge bonds, not three. The structure has two bridging Cl atoms connecting the two Al atoms. ‚úó

**Statement III ‚ÄĒ Correct:** Boron has an exceptionally high melting point of **2349 K** and is very hard, making it a valuable refractory material. ‚úď

Correct statements: I and III only.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 43

text: Magnesium is burnt in air to form A and B. When B is hydrolysed, C and D are formed. D is the reactant in the manufacture of nitric acid by Ostwald's process. What is C?
explanation:
**Step 1:** Mg burnt in air:
$$2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO} \ (\textbf{A})$$
$$3\text{Mg} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2 \ (\textbf{B, magnesium nitride})$$

**Step 2:** Hydrolysis of Mg‚āÉN‚āā (B):
$$\text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\text{Mg(OH)}_2 + 2\text{NH}_3$$
- **C = Mg(OH)‚āā** (magnesium hydroxide)
- **D = NH‚āÉ** (ammonia)

**Verification:** NH‚āÉ (D) is the starting material in the **Ostwald process** for manufacturing nitric acid:
$$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt catalyst}} 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Therefore, **C = Mg(OH)‚āā**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 44

text:
Which of the following reactions can be used to prepare diborane?

I. $$\text{BF}_3 + \text{LiAlH}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{ether}}$$
II. $$\text{BF}_3 + \text{NaH} \xrightarrow{450 \ \text{K}}$$
III. $$\text{Na}_2\text{B}_4\text{O}_7 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow$$
IV. $$\text{NaBH}_4 + \text{I}_2 \rightarrow$$
explanation:
**Reaction I ‚ÄĒ Correct:**
$$4\text{BF}_3 + 3\text{LiAlH}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{ether}} 2\text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 3\text{LiF} + 3\text{AlF}_3$$ ‚úď

**Reaction II ‚ÄĒ Correct (Industrial method):**
$$2\text{BF}_3 + 6\text{NaH} \xrightarrow{450 \ \text{K}} \text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 6\text{NaF}$$ ‚úď

**Reaction III ‚ÄĒ Incorrect:**
$$\text{Na}_2\text{B}_4\text{O}_7 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NaOH} + \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3$$
This gives **boric acid**, not diborane. ‚úó

**Reaction IV ‚ÄĒ Correct (Lab method):**
$$2\text{NaBH}_4 + \text{I}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{diglyme}} \text{B}_2\text{H}_6 + 2\text{NaI} + \text{H}_2\uparrow$$ ‚úď

Therefore, reactions **I, II, and IV** can be used to prepare diborane.
name: s and p Block Elements Item 45

text: The number of P‚ÄďOH bonds present in pyrophosphoric acid and hypophosphoric acid respectively is:
explanation:
**Pyrophosphoric acid (H‚āĄP‚āāO‚āá):**
Structure: Two H‚āÉPO‚āĄ units joined by a P‚ÄďO‚ÄďP bridge (losing one H‚āāO)
- Contains **4 P‚ÄďOH bonds** (4 acidic H atoms ‚Üí tetrabasic acid) ‚úď

**Hypophosphoric acid (H‚āĄP‚āāO‚āÜ):**
Structure: Two phosphorus atoms directly bonded (P‚ÄďP bond), each with two OH groups and one =O group
- Contains **4 P‚ÄďOH bonds** (4 acidic H atoms ‚Üí tetrabasic acid) ‚úď

Both acids are tetrabasic with **4 P‚ÄďOH bonds** each.

Therefore: Pyrophosphoric acid = **4**, Hypophosphoric acid = **4**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 46

text:
In how many of the following sulphur compounds is the oxidation state of the sulphur atom +6?

$$\text{H}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8, \ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_5, \ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_3, \ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4, \ \text{H}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_7, \ \text{SO}_2\text{Cl}_2, \ \text{SOCl}_2$$
explanation:
Calculating oxidation state of S in each compound:

- **H‚āāS‚āāO‚āą** (Marshall's acid): S = **+6** ‚úď (peroxodisulphuric acid)
- **H‚āāSO‚āÖ** (Caro's acid): S = **+6** ‚úď (peroxomonosulphuric acid)
- **H‚āāSO‚āÉ** (sulphurous acid): S = **+4** ‚úó
- **H‚āāSO‚āĄ** (sulphuric acid): S = **+6** ‚úď
- **H‚āāS‚āāO‚āá** (pyrosulphuric acid): S = **+6** ‚úď
- **SO‚āāCl‚āā** (sulphuryl chloride): $$x + 2(-1) + 2(-2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +6$$ ‚úď... wait, $$x - 2 - 4 = 0$$ ‚Üí x = +6 ‚úď
- **SOCl‚āā** (thionyl chloride): $$x + (-2) + 2(-1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = +4$$ ‚úó

Compounds with S in **+6** state: H‚āāS‚āāO‚āą, H‚āāSO‚āÖ, H‚āāS‚āāO‚āá, SO‚āāCl‚āā = **4 compounds**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 47

text: A compound (MO‚āā) of a Group 1 element (M) hydrolyses to form M‚Āļ, OH‚ĀĽ and Y. When X reacts with I‚āā in basic medium, the products formed are I‚ĀĽ, water and Y. Then X and Y are respectively:
explanation:
**Step 1:** Hydrolysis of MO‚āā (superoxide of Group 1 metal):
$$2\text{MO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{M}^+ + 2\text{OH}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{X}) + \text{O}_2(\text{Y})$$

So **X = H‚āāO‚āā** and a preliminary **Y = O‚āā**

**Step 2:** X (H‚āāO‚āā) reacts with I‚āā in basic medium:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{I}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{I}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2$$

H‚āāO‚āā acts as a **reducing agent** and is oxidised to **O‚āā (Y)**.

Therefore: **X = H‚āāO‚āā** and **Y = O‚āā**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 48

text: Which one of the following statements is NOT correct regarding phosphine (PH‚āÉ)?
options: It reacts with CuSO‚āĄ solution to form CuHPO‚āĄ
It is formed by the reaction of Ca‚āÉP‚āā with HCl
It is used in smoke screens
explanation:
**Statement (b) is incorrect:** When phosphine (PH‚āÉ) is passed through CuSO‚āĄ solution, it does **not** form CuHPO‚āĄ. Instead, it forms a **black precipitate of cupric phosphide (Cu‚āÉP‚āā)**:

$$3\text{CuSO}_4 + 2\text{PH}_3 \rightarrow \text{Cu}_3\text{P}_2\downarrow(\text{black}) + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

All other statements are correct:
- PH‚āÉ is a weak base (weaker than NH‚āÉ) ‚úď
- Ca‚āÉP‚āā + 6HCl ‚Üí 2PH‚āÉ + 3CaCl‚āā ‚úď
- PH‚āÉ is used in smoke screens (Holme's signals) ‚úď
name: s and p Block Elements Item 49

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

i. Sulphuric acid is manufactured by the contact process.
ii. SO‚āÉ dissolves in H‚āāSO‚āĄ to form pyrosulphuric acid.
iii. H‚āāSO‚āĄ is used in the manufacture of fertilisers such as ammonium sulphate and superphosphate.
iv. In the reaction S + 2H‚āāSO‚āĄ(conc.) ‚Üí 3SO‚āā + 2H‚āāO, H‚āāSO‚āĄ is oxidised to SO‚āā.
explanation:
**Statement (i) ‚ÄĒ Correct:** H‚āāSO‚āĄ is manufactured by the **contact process** (oxidation of SO‚āā to SO‚āÉ using V‚āāO‚āÖ catalyst, then absorption in H‚āāSO‚āĄ). ‚úď

**Statement (ii) ‚ÄĒ Correct:**
$$\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_7 \ (\text{pyrosulphuric acid/oleum})$$ ‚úď

**Statement (iii) ‚ÄĒ Correct:** H‚āāSO‚āĄ is used extensively in making ammonium sulphate (NH‚āĄ)‚āāSO‚āĄ and calcium superphosphate fertilisers. ‚úď

**Statement (iv) ‚ÄĒ Incorrect:** In the reaction:
$$\text{S} + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\text{(conc.)} \rightarrow 3\text{SO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- S goes from 0 ‚Üí +4 (oxidised)
- S in H‚āāSO‚āĄ goes from +6 ‚Üí +4 (**reduced**, not oxidised)
- H‚āāSO‚āĄ acts as an **oxidising agent** (gets reduced to SO‚āā) ‚úó

Correct statements: **(i), (ii), (iii) only**
name: s and p Block Elements Item 50
